text: Welcome to another video in this series of Versius robotic thoracic surgery from Sven Seifert and his team in Chemnitz in Germany.
So here we're doing quite a small case really.
This is a right upper lobe nodule of unknown histology.
The mission is to go and get it out, maybe look at some lymph nodes and main mission, find histology.
So because it's a smaller procedure, Sven has elected to just use four ports rather than the usual five.
He's got two Versius arms, which you can see by the purple and the cyan instrument units, top right, top left.
And as you can see, he's got really good view, nice lung collapse using CO2, but lots of adhesions, poor guy.
So happens to us all, but he's doing a really nice job with the Versius system, combined with a good bedside assistant to take down these adhesions.
So he's got a fenestrated grasper in his right hand,

and a fenestrated maryland in his left hand the left hand instrument does have energy the right hand does not but because these looked a little bit vascular sven is using his assistant to bring in a harmonic case and previous to that some shears with energy but a harmonic case i think was a really nice choice for these adhesions does a great job of being nice and hemostatic

as we all know in robotic surgery if you get a lot of blood in the field the camera just gets darker you don't see as well so so well done for him for doing that so he's using his right hand it's a really good idea to get a rolled up swab for all robotic operations really it's actually better than using a sucker because we've got co2 to push the lung down
And so if you bring a sucker in, that actually sucks the CO2 out and brings the lung up quite a lot.
So actually using rolled up swabs is a much better idea.
And often there isn't that much blood.
And as you can see, Sven is peeling away the loose adhesions and using the harmonic case for the bigger adhesions.
So now he's going to two arms to now use with his left arm.
He's using his swab.
It's quite a nice idea not to grasp the lung, especially if the lung is going to stay in.
So he's just padding with the swab, which is a really good technique to stop air leak or tears.
Now we see the Maryland and fenestrated grasper identifying the nodule, which is fairly peripherally on the lung surface at the apex of the right upper lobe.
You can see it was quite important to get those adhesions down so we can do a good firing to the other side.
And the robotic fenestrated grasper is just lifting up the nodule so we can get a good firing.
So the portsy's got
are two 5mm ports, allied medical 5mm ports for the robotic arms and then he's needed a 12mm port for the stapler and a 10mm port for the endoscope, so four port technique.
That was a Cavidian Purple Tri Stapler 60, which is a good choice and is being fired by his bedside assistant.
So in this video, I think the endoscope was really quite low in the chest and the arms were a fair bit higher.
Just then experimenting with arm positions and also having the expert knowledge of knowing exactly the target he's going for.
So that was a really good idea.
It allowed good arm movement.
He got around the chest really nicely with the five mil arms to get out this nodule.
And out it comes in a bag.
We'll just have to extend the incision just a little bit to get that out not too much And it's always good to just have a little look around to see if we can find any lymph nodes.

the assistant to do a bit of retracting which I thought was a good idea so and now the assistant again just doing a little bit of adhesiolysis and having a look for some lymph nodes coming to the back as well to see if might be good to get some access to the station 7 area the subclinal station quite a lot of adhesions and
As we come to the close of this video, it actually turned out that this nodule was benign.
It was pulmonary necrosis and not malignancy, which is really good news.
Obviously, that was on final histology.
So Sven was obviously very careful, having a look and taking some lymph nodes where possible as well, just in case it had been malignant.

using the two arms nicely.
